walkthrough.title: Gather the Components
walkthrough.text: Let's get Fox in the Forest set up. You will need the 33 cards in three suits: Bells, Keys, and Moons, each numbered one through eleven. You also need the scoring tokens and the score track. Set the score track on the table between both players.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal
walkthrough.text: Give all 33 cards a good shuffle. Deal 13 cards face down to each player. That leaves seven cards remaining. One of those seven will become the trump card, and the other six form the draw deck.

walkthrough.title: Reveal the Trump Card
walkthrough.text: Take the top card of the remaining pile and place it face up on the table. This card determines the trump suit for the round. Whatever suit this card shows, that suit beats all other suits when played. Place the remaining six cards face down as the draw deck.

walkthrough.title: Note the Card Abilities
walkthrough.text: Before you start, remember that odd-numbered cards have special abilities. The one, three, five, seven, nine, and eleven each do something unique when played. We will cover these in the rules section. Keep your player aid card handy for quick reference.

walkthrough.title: Place Scoring Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player places their scoring token on position zero of the score track. You will move these tokens as you score points across multiple rounds. The game continues until someone reaches 21 points.

walkthrough.title: Determine Who Leads
walkthrough.text: The non-dealer leads the first trick. Pick up your 13 cards and look at your hand. You will play 13 tricks this round. Ready to learn the rules? Let's go.

summary.title: Gather the Components
bullets: 33 cards in 3 suits (Bells, Keys, Moons), numbered 1-11
Scoring tokens + score track between players

bullets: Shuffle all 33 cards
Deal 13 cards face down to each player
7 cards remain for trump + draw deck

bullets: Flip top card face up = trump suit for the round
Trump suit beats non-trump when played
Remaining 6 cards form the draw deck

bullets: Odd-numbered cards (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11) have special abilities
Keep player aid card handy for reference

summary.title: Place Scoring Tokens

summary.title: Determine Who Leads
bullets: Non-dealer leads the first trick
13 tricks played per round

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Fox in the Forest is a two-player trick-taking card game with a unique twist. Winning too many tricks is greedy and scores you zero points. You need to find the sweet spot, winning just enough tricks to score well without getting punished for being too dominant.

walkthrough.title: Playing a Trick
walkthrough.text: The leader plays any card from their hand. The other player must follow suit if they can. If you cannot follow suit, you may play any card from your hand, including a trump card. The highest card of the led suit wins the trick, unless a trump card was played, in which case the highest trump wins.

walkthrough.title: After a Trick
walkthrough.text: The winner of the trick collects both cards and places them face down in their trick pile. The winner then leads the next trick. Continue until all 13 tricks have been played.

walkthrough.title: The Trump Suit
walkthrough.text: The face-up card next to the draw deck determines the trump suit for the round. Trump cards beat any non-trump card regardless of number. If both players play trump, the higher trump wins. The trump suit adds a layer of strategy to every trick.

walkthrough.title: Card Abilities: Swan and Fox
walkthrough.text: Let's go through the special abilities on odd-numbered cards. The one is the Swan. If you play it and lose the trick, you lead the next trick instead of the winner. The three is the Fox. When you play it, you may swap the face-up trump card with a card from your hand.

walkthrough.title: Card Abilities: Woodcutter and Treasure
walkthrough.text: The five is the Woodcutter. When you play it, draw one card from the draw deck, then discard one card face down. This lets you improve your hand. The seven is the Treasure. Whoever wins the trick containing the seven scores one bonus point.

walkthrough.title: Card Abilities: Witch and Monarch
walkthrough.text: The nine is the Witch. If you lead with the Witch, it counts as trump regardless of its actual suit. This is a powerful surprise play. The eleven is the Monarch. You must play it if it is your only card of the led suit. This is a restriction, not a benefit.

walkthrough.title: The Scoring Table
walkthrough.text: Here is where Fox in the Forest gets interesting. Your score depends on how many tricks you win. Zero to three tricks is Humble and scores six points. Four tricks scores one point. Five tricks scores two. Six tricks scores three. Seven to nine tricks is Victorious and scores six points. But ten to thirteen tricks is Greedy and scores zero points.

walkthrough.title: The Greedy Trap
walkthrough.text: Let's emphasize the greedy rule because it changes everything. If you win ten or more tricks out of thirteen, you score zero for the round. This means your opponent can deliberately feed you tricks to push you into greed territory. It is not just about winning tricks. It is about winning the right number of tricks.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: At the end of each round, count your tricks and score accordingly. Move your scoring token on the track. Shuffle all cards, redeal, and play another round. The first player to reach 21 points wins the game. Most games take three to four rounds.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 2-player trick-taking game
Winning too many tricks = greedy = 0 points
Find the scoring sweet spot

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Forest
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Fox in the Forest! In this tutorial, we will walk through several tricks between two example players, Alice and Bob. Alice is the non-dealer and will lead the first trick. The trump suit is Bells.

walkthrough.title: Trick One: A Simple Lead
walkthrough.text: Alice leads with the eight of Moons. Bob has Moons in his hand, so he must follow suit. He plays the ten of Moons. Ten beats eight, so Bob wins the trick. Bob collects both cards and will lead the next trick. Simple so far.

walkthrough.title: Trick Two: Playing Trump
walkthrough.text: Bob leads with the six of Keys. Alice has no Keys in her hand, so she is free to play any card. She plays the two of Bells, which is the trump suit. Even though two is a low number, trump beats non-trump. Alice wins the trick.

walkthrough.title: Trick Three: The Swan
walkthrough.text: Alice leads with the one of Moons, the Swan. Bob plays the four of Moons. Bob wins because four beats one. But the Swan's ability triggers. Even though Alice lost the trick, she gets to lead the next trick instead of Bob. The Swan gives you control of the lead.

walkthrough.title: Trick Four: The Fox
walkthrough.text: Alice leads the three of Bells, the Fox. The Fox's ability lets Alice swap the face-up trump card with a card from her hand. She swaps a weak card for the trump card, improving her hand. Then Bob follows suit and the trick resolves normally.

walkthrough.title: Trick Five: The Witch Surprise
walkthrough.text: Alice leads the nine of Keys, the Witch. Even though it is a Keys card, the Witch counts as trump when led. Bob must follow with Keys if he has them. But the Witch is treated as the highest possible trump for winning purposes. Alice wins the trick.

walkthrough.title: Counting Tricks at Midgame
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and count. After several tricks, Alice has won five and Bob has won four. Both are in scoring range. But Alice needs to be careful. If she wins five more, she will hit ten total, which is greedy territory and scores zero.

walkthrough.title: The Greedy Trap in Action
walkthrough.text: Bob realizes Alice is winning a lot of tricks. He starts deliberately losing tricks by playing low cards when he leads. Alice wins three more tricks, pushing her to eight. She is still safe in the Victorious range. But two more wins would be dangerous.

walkthrough.title: Alice Adjusts Her Strategy
walkthrough.text: Alice recognizes the danger. She starts playing low cards and avoiding trump to stop winning tricks. She intentionally loses the next two tricks. Her final count is eight tricks, landing in the Victorious zone for six points. Smart play.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Being Too Greedy
walkthrough.text: In a different game, Alice does not watch her trick count. She plays aggressively, winning trick after trick with powerful trump cards. She ends the round with eleven tricks. That is Greedy. She scores zero points while Bob scores six for the Humble category with only two tricks won.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Count Your Tricks
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is simple. Always count how many tricks you and your opponent have won. The scoring table is the whole game. Aim for seven to nine tricks if you are ahead, or zero to three if you are behind. The middle ground of four to six tricks scores poorly.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring Abilities
walkthrough.text: Bob has the Woodcutter in his hand for several tricks but never plays it. He forgets that the five lets him draw a card and discard one, which could have improved his hand dramatically. Meanwhile, Alice uses every ability to her advantage.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Your Special Cards
walkthrough.text: Do not forget about card abilities. The Fox, Woodcutter, Swan, and Witch are powerful tools that can swing the game. Plan when to play them for maximum impact rather than letting them sit unused in your hand.

walkthrough.title: End of Round Scoring
walkthrough.text: All 13 tricks are done. Alice won eight tricks for six points. Bob won five tricks for two points. Move the scoring tokens on the track. Shuffle all cards, deal a new hand of 13 each, flip a new trump card, and start another round.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have played through your first round of Fox in the Forest! You have seen how tricks work, how trump changes the game, and how the greedy scoring keeps both players honest. Remember to count your tricks, use your abilities, and watch for the greed trap.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy on managing your trick count.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all card abilities.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest mistakes new Fox in the Forest players make. First, winning as many tricks as possible without watching the count. Second, ignoring card abilities and playing only by the numbers. Third, not tracking your opponent's trick count, which means you cannot push them toward greed. Keep these in mind from your very first round.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Target Range
walkthrough.text: Before the first trick, decide your target range based on your hand strength. If you have strong cards and trump, aim for seven to nine tricks, which is the Victorious zone and scores six points. If your hand is weak, pivot to the Humble strategy, aiming for zero to three tricks, which also scores six points. The worst spot is the middle. Four to six tricks scores only one to three points.

walkthrough.title: Control the Lead
walkthrough.text: Leading a trick gives you a significant advantage because you choose the suit. The Swan is incredibly powerful because it lets you regain the lead even when you lose a trick. If you have the Swan, play it strategically to take back control at a key moment rather than wasting it early.

walkthrough.title: Trump Management
walkthrough.text: Trump cards are your most powerful weapons, but using them all early leaves you vulnerable later. Save at least one trump for the end of the round when you need to win or lose a specific trick. The Fox ability to swap the trump card can also improve your trump holdings if used wisely.

walkthrough.title: Force Your Opponent into Greed
walkthrough.text: If your opponent is winning lots of tricks, you can deliberately feed them more by playing low cards and letting them win. Push them above nine tricks and they score zero. This is a legitimate and effective strategy, especially when your hand is weak. Going Humble for six points while forcing them Greedy for zero is a massive swing.

walkthrough.title: Use the Woodcutter
walkthrough.text: The Woodcutter lets you draw from the deck and discard a card. This is one of the most flexible abilities in the game. Use it to dig for trump cards, dump a card you do not want, or adjust your hand to match your target trick range. Do not sleep on this ability.

walkthrough.title: Track Everything
walkthrough.text: Good Fox in the Forest players track three things constantly: how many tricks each player has won, which high cards have been played, and which suits their opponent might be short on. This information tells you when to push for more tricks, when to stop winning, and when your opponent might be forced to play trump.

walkthrough.title: Adapt Mid-Round
walkthrough.text: Your initial plan might not survive contact with the cards. If you aimed for Victorious but your opponent is playing perfectly to force you into Greed, pivot to a different approach. Flexibility is key. The best players adjust their target range multiple times during a single round based on how tricks are falling.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't win tricks blindly — watch the count
Don't ignore card abilities — they swing games
Track opponent's trick count to exploit the greed rule

4-6 tricks is the worst scoring zone (1-3 pts)

walkthrough.title: The Humble Gambit
walkthrough.text: One of the most powerful strategies is the deliberate Humble play. When your hand is weak, lean into it. Play your lowest cards, avoid trump, and aim for zero to three tricks. Scoring six points for Humble is just as good as scoring six for Victorious. The trick is committing early and not accidentally winning tricks you do not want.

walkthrough.title: Swan Lock Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Swan gives you the lead even when you lose a trick. Use this to create a "swan lock" where you repeatedly lead with cards that force your opponent into unfavorable positions. Lead with a suit your opponent is short on, forcing them to use trump or give you information about their hand.

walkthrough.title: Trump Timing
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think of trump cards as a limited resource to be deployed at the perfect moment. In the early tricks, gathering information about your opponent's hand is more valuable than winning tricks. Save your highest trumps for the last three to four tricks when you know exactly what you need. A well-timed trump can guarantee your final trick count lands in the right zone.

walkthrough.title: The Witch as a Lead Weapon
walkthrough.text: The Witch counts as trump when led, making it one of the most dangerous opening plays. Lead with the Witch when you need to guarantee a trick win and when your opponent likely has that suit in hand. Because they must follow suit if able, they cannot counter with trump from a different suit.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Pay close attention to what your opponent takes from the draw pile when the Woodcutter is played, and what suits they cannot follow. Every piece of information narrows down their hand. If they cannot follow Keys in trick four, every future Keys lead is guaranteed to give them a choice between trumping or discarding.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Trick Manipulation
walkthrough.text: In the last three or four tricks, you should know roughly what your opponent holds. Count the tricks carefully. If you are at seven and your opponent is at six, you have scored six points in the Victorious range. Your goal in the remaining tricks is to avoid winning three more. Play your lowest remaining cards and let your opponent take the final tricks.

walkthrough.title: Fox Card Timing
walkthrough.text: The Fox lets you swap the trump card with one from your hand. The optimal time to use this is when the current trump card is high and useful for your strategy. Swap out a card you do not need and gain a strong trump. Early Fox plays give you more tricks to benefit from the improved hand.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Round Scoring Strategy
walkthrough.text: Remember the game is played to 21 points across multiple rounds. If you are at 15 points and your opponent is at 8, you only need six more points. A single Victorious or Humble round wins the game. Play conservatively and aim for a guaranteed scoring zone rather than risking a Greedy zero.

bullets: Deliberately aim for 0-3 tricks with a weak hand
Humble = 6 pts, same as Victorious
Commit early, avoid accidental trick wins

summary.title: Trump Timing

Well-timed trump guarantees target trick count

entries.definition: A single exchange where both players play one card. The leader plays first, the follower plays second. The winner collects both cards and leads the next trick.
Playing the first card of a trick. The leader chooses any card from their hand, setting the suit that the follower must match if able.
Playing a card of the same suit as the led card. You must follow suit if you have a card of that suit in your hand. If you cannot follow, you may play any card.
The suit determined by the face-up card at the start of the round. Trump cards beat any non-trump card. If both cards are trump, the higher number wins.
One of the three card suits in Fox in the Forest, depicted with bell icons.
One of the three card suits in Fox in the Forest, depicted with key icons.
One of the three card suits in Fox in the Forest, depicted with crescent moon icons.
Special ability card. If you play the Swan and lose the trick, you lead the next trick instead of the winner. Gives you control of the lead even when losing.
Special ability card. When you play the Fox, you may exchange the face-up trump card with a card from your hand. Useful for improving your trump holdings.
Special ability card. When you play the Woodcutter, draw one card from the draw deck and then discard one card face down. Allows you to improve your hand.
Special ability card. Whoever wins the trick containing the Treasure scores one bonus point added to their round score.

Special ability card. When led, the Witch counts as trump regardless of its printed suit. A powerful surprise play that forces opponents to follow the Witch's printed suit while the Witch wins as trump.
Special ability card. You must play the Monarch if it is your only card of the led suit. This is a restriction rather than a benefit.
Scoring category for winning zero to three tricks in a round. Scores six points. A deliberate strategy when your hand is weak.
Scoring category for winning seven to nine tricks in a round. Scores six points. The standard target for players with strong hands.
Scoring category for winning ten to thirteen tricks in a round. Scores zero points. The penalty for dominating too much. Opponents can force you into this range.
The six remaining cards after dealing and revealing the trump card. Used by the Woodcutter ability. Not otherwise drawn from during the round.
The track where both players mark their cumulative score across rounds. First player to 21 points wins the game.
The face-up card next to the draw deck that determines the trump suit for the round. Can be swapped by the Fox ability.
A complete set of 13 tricks played with a fresh deal. The game consists of multiple rounds until a player reaches 21 points.
The number of tricks a player has won so far in the round. Constantly tracking this is essential for making strategic decisions and avoiding the Greedy trap.
